questions.question: According to longitudinal Dutch research, what is the average friendship duration?
questions.explanation: Dutch longitudinal study tracking thousands of friendships found average duration ~17 years, including brief friendships (few months), medium friendships (5-10 years—most common), and long friendships (decades). This is an AVERAGE, meaning many friendships last much shorter than 17 years—which is completely normal, not failure. Understanding this statistic reduces shame about friendships that end in 2, 5, or 10 years; you're experiencing statistically normal relationship evolution, not being 'bad at friendship.'

questions.question: After 7 years, what percentage of friendships have ended or drifted significantly?
questions.explanation: After 7 years, 50% of friendships have ended or drifted significantly—the 'seven-year half-life.' This timeframe captures when major life changes typically occur (career shifts, moves, marriage, parenthood, new priorities), and friendships either adapt or don't. If a friendship survives 7 years, it's significantly more likely to become long-term. This normalization is crucial: HALF of friendships don't make it past 7 years, meaning you're not uniquely bad at maintaining relationships—you're experiencing universal patterns.

questions.question: What percentage of friendship endings occur through gradual drift rather than dramatic conflict or betrayal?
questions.explanation: 80%+ of friendships end through 'dissolution through drift'—gradual reduction in contact frequency until relationship fades—NOT dramatic conflict or betrayal. Both people get busy with life demands, initiating contact feels like extra work, gaps between communication widen, and eventually 'we should get together' but never do. Most friendships don't end with painful confrontations; they just fade through normal life changes (geographic distance 46%, life stage divergence 32%, value/interest drift 23%, conflict only 15%). Understanding this prevents shame: no one's the villain, life got in the way.

questions.question: According to the Proximity-Energy-Timing triangle, when is a struggling friendship likely ending and okay to let go?

options.text: When all three factors (proximity, energy, timing) are aligned
When two of three factors are aligned
When only one or none of the factors are aligned and circumstances unlikely to change
Never—always keep trying regardless of circumstances

questions.explanation: When assessing the triangle: ✅✅✅ All three aligned = friendship sustainable; ✅✅❌ Two aligned = adaptation required but possible; ✅❌❌ One aligned = likely ending unless circumstances change; ❌❌❌ None aligned = has run natural course, okay to let go. Example: Friend 1,000 miles away (proximity failed), both overwhelmed with life (energy failed), different life stages (timing failed—but same age so partial alignment) = 1/3 factors aligned. This isn't personal failure; circumstances don't support close friendship right now. Permission to accept reality and let friendship drift without guilt.

questions.question: If friendship loss is triggering prolonged depression, intense rumination preventing daily function, or patterns of repeated relationship 'failures' causing significant distress, what is the appropriate next step?

options.text: Accept that you're just bad at friendships and stop trying
Practice more gratitude reframing from this lesson
Consider therapy to process friendship grief, explore relationship patterns, and develop healthier attachment strategies—friendship loss is real loss deserving support
Force yourself into new friendships immediately to replace the loss

questions.explanation: These symptoms—prolonged depression, rumination preventing function, repeated distressing relationship patterns—suggest professional support would be helpful. Therapy can process friendship grief (loss is real and deserves compassionate support), explore patterns (why do friendships repeatedly end in similar ways?), and develop healthier attachment strategies if unresolved attachment wounds are present. Friendship loss is genuine loss, and seeking professional support isn't weakness—it's self-care. The course provides grieving frameworks, but clinical-level distress or entrenched patterns benefit from specialized therapeutic intervention beyond self-help.

title: Seasonal Friendships & Letting Go — Honoring All Relationship Seasons - Quiz
